title: Internal dispute resolution — Purpose and scope
text: This regulatory guide sets out ASIC's expectations and requirements for the internal dispute resolution (IDR) procedures of financial firms. All Australian financial services (AFS) licensees, Australian credit licensees, and unlicensed product issuers and secondary sellers must have IDR procedures that meet ASIC's requirements.

title: Maximum IDR timeframes — Standard complaints
text: Financial firms must provide an IDR response to a complainant no later than 30 calendar days after receiving the complaint. This timeframe applies to all complaints unless a different maximum timeframe applies under paragraph RG 271.56.

title: Maximum IDR timeframes — Specific complaint types
text: Different maximum IDR timeframes apply for: (a) superannuation trustee complaints other than death benefit distribution complaints — 45 calendar days; (b) superannuation death benefit distribution complaints — 90 calendar days; (c) credit-related complaints involving default notices — 21 calendar days; (d) complaints involving hardship notices or requests to postpone enforcement — 21 calendar days.

title: Written IDR response requirements
text: The IDR response must be in writing and must include: (a) the outcome of the complaint and the reasons for the decision; (b) information about the complainant's right to take the complaint to AFCA if dissatisfied; (c) the contact details for AFCA.

title: Identifying complaints
text: An expression of dissatisfaction made to or about an organisation, related to its products, services, staff or the handling of a complaint, where a response or resolution is explicitly or implicitly expected or legally required, is a complaint and must be dealt with under the firm's IDR procedures.

title: Acknowledging complaints
text: Financial firms must acknowledge receipt of a complaint to the complainant in writing within one business day of receiving the complaint, or as soon as practicable. The acknowledgment should include a unique complaint reference number and expected timeframes for resolution.

title: Systemic issues and serious contraventions
text: Financial firms must have processes for identifying, recording, reporting, and acting on systemic issues arising from complaints. A systemic issue is an issue that has implications beyond an individual complaint, including issues that affect or have the potential to affect multiple consumers.

title: Data collection and reporting
text: Financial firms must collect and report IDR data to ASIC, including the number of complaints received, open and closed, the issues and outcomes, and the time taken to close complaints. This data helps ASIC monitor compliance and identify emerging consumer issues.

title: Licensing: Internal and external dispute resolution — Hardship
text: When a consumer notifies their financial firm of financial difficulty, the firm should consider whether it is appropriate to: (a) postpone or stay any enforcement action; (b) extend the period of the loan; (c) reduce the consumer's repayments; (d) accept a lump sum payment; or (e) agree to any other reasonable arrangement the consumer proposes.

title: Credit licensing: Responsible lending conduct — Overview
text: This guide sets out our expectations for how credit licensees and their representatives should meet their responsible lending obligations under the National Consumer Credit Protection Act 2009 (National Credit Act). Licensees must not enter into or suggest a credit contract that is unsuitable for the consumer.

title: Responsible lending — Reasonable inquiries about financial situation
text: A credit licensee must make reasonable inquiries about the consumer's financial situation, including their income, existing debts and financial obligations, and living expenses. The licensee must take reasonable steps to verify the consumer's financial situation.

title: Responsible lending — Assessment of suitability
text: A credit contract is unsuitable if, at the time of assessment: (a) it does not meet the consumer's requirements and objectives; or (b) the consumer could not comply with the financial obligations under the contract, or could only comply with substantial hardship.

title: Client review and remediation conducted by advice licensees
text: This guide sets out ASIC's expectations for AFS licensees when conducting a client review and remediation program. Licensees should ensure that affected clients are identified, assessed, and compensated in a timely, fair, and transparent manner.

title: Remediation — Calculating losses and compensation
text: When calculating compensation for affected clients, the licensee should compare the client's actual position with the position the client would have been in had the relevant conduct or breach not occurred. Compensation should restore the client to the position they would have been in.

title: External dispute resolution — AFCA scheme requirements
text: AFCA is approved by the Minister as the single external dispute resolution scheme for financial complaints. Financial firms that are required to be a member of AFCA must comply with AFCA's Rules and operational guidelines.

title: AFCA monetary limits and compensation caps
text: AFCA can consider complaints involving claims up to $1,085,000 for most complaint types. The maximum compensation AFCA can award is: (a) $559,600 for complaints about credit, banking, finance, and insurance; (b) $250,000 for complaints about investments and advice; (c) $40,500 for complaints about general insurance brokers.

title: Breach reporting by AFS licensees and credit licensees
text: AFS licensees and credit licensees must report significant breaches and likely significant breaches to ASIC. A breach is significant if it constitutes a contravention of a core obligation, or if the breach results in material loss or damage to clients.

title: Breach reporting — Notification timeframes
text: Licensees must lodge a breach report with ASIC within 30 calendar days of becoming aware the breach is a reportable situation. The report must include a description of the breach, the number of affected clients, and the steps taken or proposed to address the breach.

title: Product design and distribution obligations
text: Issuers and distributors of financial products must comply with the design and distribution obligations in Part 7.8A of the Corporations Act. These obligations require issuers to design products that are likely to be consistent with the likely objectives, financial situation and needs of the consumers in the target market.

title: Target market determination — Requirements
text: An issuer must make a target market determination (TMD) for each financial product before it is distributed. The TMD must describe the target market, any conditions or restrictions on distribution, and events or circumstances that would reasonably suggest the TMD is no longer appropriate.

title: Section 13 — Duty of utmost good faith
text: A contract of insurance is a contract based on the utmost good faith and there is implied in such a contract a provision requiring each party to it to act towards the other party, in respect of any matter arising under or in relation to it, with the utmost good faith.

title: Section 54 — Insurer may not refuse to pay claims in certain circumstances
text: Where the effect of a contract of insurance would, but for this section, be that the insurer may refuse to pay a claim, wholly or in part, by reason of some act or omission of the insured, the insurer may not refuse to pay the claim by reason only of that act or omission if the insured proves that no part of the loss was caused by the act or omission.

title: Section 14 — Parties not to rely on provisions except in utmost good faith
text: If reliance by a party to a contract of insurance on a provision of the contract would be to fail to act with the utmost good faith, the party may not rely on the provision.

title: Section 72 — Obligation to assess unsuitability
text: A credit provider must not enter into a credit contract with a consumer, or increase the credit limit of an existing contract, unless the provider has assessed whether the contract is unsuitable for the consumer and determined it is not unsuitable.

title: Section 130 — Hardship changes
text: If a debtor is unable to meet their obligations under a credit contract due to hardship (illness, unemployment, or other reasonable cause), the debtor may give the credit provider a hardship notice. The credit provider must respond within 21 days and must consider making changes to the contract.

title: Section 133 — Enforcement proceedings and hardship
text: A credit provider must not begin enforcement proceedings against a debtor in relation to a credit contract if a hardship notice has been given and the credit provider has not yet responded to the notice.

title: Section 12CB — Unconscionable conduct in connection with financial services
text: A person must not, in trade or commerce, in connection with the supply or possible supply of financial services to a person, engage in conduct that is, in all the circumstances, unconscionable. The court may have regard to the relative strengths of the bargaining positions of the parties.

title: Section 12DA — Misleading or deceptive conduct
text: A person must not, in trade or commerce, engage in conduct in relation to financial services that is misleading or deceptive or is likely to mislead or deceive.

title: Chapter 32 — When things go wrong: Complaint handling
text: We will handle complaints in a way that is consistent with ASIC regulatory standards. We will acknowledge your complaint promptly and provide you with information about our complaint handling process, including expected timeframes and your option to take your complaint to AFCA.

title: Chapter 40 — Financial difficulty: Working with you
text: If you tell us you are in financial difficulty, we will try to help you. We will work with you to find a sustainable solution, which could include changing your repayment amount, extending the term of your loan, or providing a repayment pause.

title: Chapter 47 — Guarantees: Providing information
text: Before you sign a guarantee, we will give you information about the risks of providing a guarantee, including the potential liability you may face. We will encourage you to get independent legal and financial advice before signing.

title: Section 7 — Claims handling
text: We will handle claims promptly, fairly and transparently. For most claims we will make a decision within 10 business days of receiving all the information we need. If we need more time, we will let you know the reason for the delay and keep you informed of the progress.

title: Section 8 — Claims: Financial hardship
text: If you tell us you are experiencing financial hardship, we will consider whether we can provide you with support, such as fast-tracking your claim, making an advance payment, or providing interim accommodation or vehicle hire.

title: Section 9 — Claims: Cash settlements
text: If we offer a cash settlement instead of repair or replacement, the cash settlement amount will be fair and reasonable and will be based on the cost to repair or replace the insured property with property of a similar type, age and condition.

title: Section 9 — Claims: Assessment principles
text: We will assess claims fairly, transparently, and in a timely manner. We will not deny a claim based on non-disclosure unless the non-disclosure is directly relevant to the claim and would have affected our decision to offer cover. We will consider all available medical evidence, including evidence from the claimant's treating practitioners.

title: APP 11 — Security of personal information
text: An APP entity that holds personal information must take such steps as are reasonable in the circumstances to protect the information from misuse, interference and loss, and from unauthorised access, modification or disclosure.
